region_of_origin: East Java, Indonesia — particularly Surabaya and Malang
food_subgroup: Braised beef soup with keluak nut paste; 1 bowl beef only ~350g (without rice); low glycaemic index

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Rawon specifically. Closest parent would be a beef soup or stew concept. Gap noted; null retained pending FoodOn expansion.
ontology.foodex2_description: East Javanese black beef soup with keluak nut (rawon) — closest_parent match within FoodEx2 beef soup category
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0GCD used as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a traditional Indonesian beef soup. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Rawon.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for Rawon or East Javanese black beef soup. Null retained.

serving.description: 1 bowl beef soup (soup + beef, without rice)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Rawon contains approximately 350g (braised beef short ribs ~150g plus black keluak broth ~200g), served without rice, prawn crackers, or salted duck egg accompaniments.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for South East Asia (2020) — Rawon component calculation: beef short ribs (USDA SR Legacy #23352) + keluak paste broth
nutrient_source.organization: FAO / INFOODS SEA; USDA SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Rawon is not listed as a single entry in FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020. Values derived from component calculation: beef short ribs (USDA SR Legacy) + keluak nut paste broth contribution, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA regional beef soup entries. Confidence medium. Core macros (energy 117 kcal, protein 8.0g, fat 6.3g, carbohydrate 2.3g per 100g) are consistent with a braised beef soup of this composition.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimated from analogous low-carbohydrate beef soup; University of Sydney GI Database — beef-based soups and stews reference; GL calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (10 × 8.05g) / 100 ≈ 0.8
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Rawon in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~10 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) the soup's very low available carbohydrate content (~2.3g per 100g, ~8g per 350g serving); (2) the dominant macronutrient being protein and fat from beef, which do not raise blood glucose; (3) comparison with other low-carbohydrate beef soups and stews in the University of Sydney GI Database which consistently score GI <15. GL per 350g serving = (10 × 8.05) / 100 = 0.8 — negligible. This estimate is consistent with the FAO/INFOODS SEA source data and the input record's stated GI ~10.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — ~850mg per 350g bowl from the broth alone
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should consume Rawon soup cautiously: the soup itself delivers ~850mg sodium per bowl (~35% of the WHO 2,300mg daily limit), and the traditional accompaniment of one salted duck egg adds a further ~1,500mg sodium, bringing the total meal sodium to ~2,350mg — exceeding the WHO daily limit in a single meal; omitting the salted duck egg and requesting reduced-salt broth are the most effective modifications.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycaemic index (~10) and negligible glycaemic load (~0.8 per bowl) due to minimal carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Rawon soup itself is well-suited for Type 2 Diabetes management — GI ~10 and GL ~0.8 per bowl are negligible; however, the traditional accompaniments of steamed rice (GL ~31 per bowl) and prawn crackers (GI ~80+, GL ~16 per 30g) substantially raise the total meal glycaemic load and should be limited or omitted by patients managing blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~9g per 350g bowl from beef short rib fat) and high sodium (~850mg per bowl)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, Rawon soup presents a moderate concern due to its saturated fat content (~2.5g per 100g, ~9g per bowl from beef short ribs) and high sodium (~850mg per bowl); leaner beef cuts such as topside or silverside can reduce saturated fat by approximately 40%, and omitting the salted duck egg accompaniment is essential to keep total meal sodium within cardiovascular-safe limits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High zinc (3.5mg per 100g) and collagen-rich broth from long-simmered short ribs
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Rawon is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients — it provides high zinc (3.5mg per 100g, significant for immune function), high protein (28g per bowl), and a collagen-rich gelatinous broth from long-simmered short ribs; moderate saturated fat (~9g per bowl) should be noted but is not a primary concern at this serving size.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rawon is a traditional East Javanese black beef soup, one of Indonesia's oldest documented dishes with references in Javanese manuscripts dating to approximately 800–900 CE. It is prepared by braising beef short ribs until tender in a jet-black broth made from fermented keluak nut (Pangium edule) paste combined with lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, kaffir lime leaf, shallots, and sweet dark soy sauce. The defining ingredient is the keluak nut paste, which gives the soup its characteristic jet-black colour and unique earthy, nutty, slightly bitter flavour profile. The broth is rich and gelatinous from the long-simmered short ribs. Per 350g bowl (soup and beef only, without accompaniments): approximately 410 kcal, 28g protein, 22g fat, 8g carbohydrate, GI ~10 (negligible), GL ~0.8, ~850mg sodium. Nutritionally, Rawon is a high-protein, moderate-fat, very low-carbohydrate dish. It is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. CLINICAL NOTE: The soup itself is T2DM-friendly (GI ~10, GL ~0.8) but the traditional accompaniments — steamed rice (GL ~31), prawn crackers (GI ~80+), and salted duck egg (~1,500mg sodium) — substantially alter the nutritional profile of the full meal. Hypertensive patients should omit the salted duck egg. SAFETY NOTE: Commercial keluak paste is safe (fully fermented and detoxified); raw keluak contains hydrogen cyanide (HCN) and must never be consumed unprocessed. Source: FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 + USDA SR Legacy component calculation. Confidence: medium.

confidence_reason: Rawon is not listed as a single composite entry in FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are derived from a component calculation using beef short ribs (USDA SR Legacy) and keluak paste broth contribution, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA regional beef soup entries. Core macros are internally consistent and plausible for this dish composition. GI is an analogous estimate (no direct measurement in University of Sydney GI Database). Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Rawon (braised beef short ribs in keluak broth) is not available as a composite dish entry in FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-14. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish preparation is not reported in the available approved sources. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed trace/negligible per USDA SR Legacy beef short rib data). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed negligible — beef short ribs and keluak paste are not meaningful sources of vitamin D per USDA SR Legacy). All core macros (protein_g 8.0, fat_g 6.3, carbohydrate_g 2.3) are non-zero and well-supported by component calculation. GI ~10 is an analogous estimate based on the soup's very low available carbohydrate content and comparison with low-carbohydrate beef soups in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Rawon exists in any approved GI source. The input record's occasion_tag of 'snack' appears incorrect for a full beef soup dish — corrected to lunch/dinner meal context. Two default portions were present in the input (400g and 350g); 350g is used as the primary serving size as it represents the soup-and-beef-only bowl without accompaniments, which is the nutritionally defined unit for this record.
